summary: O'Neil recounts a talk by Merlyna Lim about social media's role in grassroots organizing and revolution. She explores how the Arab Spring's Mohamed Bouazizi became a revolutionary symbol partly through embellished details that created a more compelling narrative. The post examines why certain stories mobilize people while others don't, connecting this to Peter Turchin's theory about 'elite overproduction' and unemployed educated classes driving social unrest. O'Neil questions whether society sympathizes more with hungry poor people or frustrated educated elites, concluding she personally worries more about the former.
key_points: Social media is effective at mobilizing people against a common enemy but poor at facilitating nuanced discussions for building lasting change
The Bouazizi narrative that sparked revolution included 'white lies' (college-educated, slapped by female officer) that made it more compelling than similar true stories
Previous self-immolations in Tunisia didn't spark revolution because 'killing yourself because you are frustrated at not having enough to eat just isn't as compelling'
Peter Turchin's theory suggests unemployed but highly educated populations ('elite overproduction') create conditions for revolution through disgruntled elite-wannabes
O'Neil personally sympathizes more with those whose food stamps were cut than with unemployed lawyers
money_quotes: Whereas social media is pretty good at energizing people to 'get rid of their enemy' at a given critical moment, and mobilize on the street, it's not that great at nuanced discussions for how to build something permanent and lasting afterwards.
Killing yourself because you are frustrated at not having enough to eat just isn't as compelling.
A large class of disgruntled elite-wannabes, often well-educated and highly capable, has been denied access to elite positions.
Does one have more sympathy for people whose foodstamps have been recently cut or for someone who got a law degree and can't find a job?
Personally, and this is maybe because I've been reading Jonathan Kozol's Savage Inequalities, I'm not as worried about the lawyer.
key_insight: Revolutionary movements require compelling narratives that resonate emotionally, and society often finds educated people's frustrated ambitions more mobilizing than pure poverty and hunger.
